title: The slow return of pleasure after years of numbness

htmlContent:
The slow return of pleasure after years of numbness

I remember the exact moment I realized I couldn't feel pleasure anymore. It wasn't dramatic. No crashing wave of grief. I was sitting on my couch, eating a piece of chocolate that I knew should taste good, and I felt nothing. Not the chocolate. Not my body. Not the warmth of the room. Just a flat, gray static where sensation used to live.

You know that feeling, don't you? That hollow place where joy, desire, and satisfaction used to reside. It's not depression exactly. It's something quieter. Something that whispers "this is fine" when everything inside you knows it's not fine at all. It's the numbness that trauma leaves in its wake, a protective armor that worked so well for so long that you forgot you were wearing it.

Here's the thing. Numbness isn't a failure. It's a solution. Your nervous system didn't go numb because something was wrong with you. It went numb because something was wrong with your environment. And it needed to survive.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, would call this a dorsal vagal shutdown. It's the most ancient part of your nervous system, the one that evolved to help you play dead when a predator was too close. Your body decided that feeling nothing was safer than feeling everything. And honestly? It was probably right at the time.

But here you are now. Still breathing. Still reading. And somewhere inside that numb body, there's a tiny flicker of wanting to feel again. That flicker is real. And it's terrifying.

Why numbness feels safer than sensation

Your brain isn't stupid. It learned that pleasure was dangerous. Maybe pleasure meant getting your hopes up and having them crushed. Maybe it meant being vulnerable and getting hurt. Maybe it meant experiencing something good and then losing it, which hurts more than never having it at all.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma gets trapped in the body. It's not just a memory in your head. It's a physical imprint, a tension pattern, a held breath, a collapsed chest. Your body remembers the danger even when your mind has moved on. And it keeps bracing for impact, even when the impact never comes.

So pleasure becomes a threat. Your system treats a warm hug the same way it would treat a punch. Both are overwhelming. Both require you to be present in your body. And your body has learned that presence equals pain.

I worked with a client once who described it perfectly. She said, "I don't know how to let good things in. My body just bounces them off like I'm wearing some kind of invisible armor." She was right. She was wearing armor. We all are. It's just that the armor gets heavy after a while, and you forget what it felt like to move without it.

Think about it. When was the last time you felt genuinely excited about something? Not anxious excitement, not the kind that feels like dread wearing a party hat. I mean the kind of excitement that makes your chest feel light and your breath come easy. If you can't remember, you're not alone. Stay with me here.

The return of pleasure isn't about forcing yourself to feel good. It's about creating enough safety for your nervous system to slowly, carefully, take off the armor. One piece at a time.

The body doesn't know how to trust yet

Here's what nobody tells you about recovery. The numbness doesn't lift all at once. It lifts in layers, like fog burning off a field. And each layer reveals something you might not be ready to feel.

When I first started trying to feel again, I didn't feel pleasure. I felt pain. Old pain. The kind that had been sitting in my body for decades, waiting for me to be strong enough to hold it. And I thought I was doing something wrong. I thought I was broken in a new way.

But Janina Fisher, a leading expert on structural dissociation, explains that this is exactly what happens. The body stores undigested experience. When you start to open to sensation, you don't just get the good stuff. You get everything. The grief you never cried. The anger you never expressed. The terror you never let yourself feel.

It's like cleaning out a closet you've been stuffing things into for years. You can't just open the door and find everything neatly organized. You find a mess. And you have to sort through it before you can find the things you actually want to keep.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helped me understand why my body learned to shut down in the first place. Why safety felt foreign. Why pleasure felt like a trap.

The return of pleasure requires you to feel the pain that numbness was protecting you from. That's the hard part. That's the part nobody talks about on Instagram. But it's also the part that makes the pleasure real when it finally comes.

Small sensations, big resistance

You don't start with orgasms and gourmet meals. You start with a warm cup of tea. The feeling of sunlight on your arm. The weight of a blanket on your legs. Tiny sensations that don't threaten your system.

And even those will feel like too much at first. Your body will want to pull away. It will want to distract you with thoughts, with phone scrolling, with anything that keeps you from being present. That's normal. That's not failure. That's your nervous system doing its job.

I remember the first time I tried to just feel my feet on the floor. I lasted about three seconds before my mind went into a full panic. My heart started racing. My chest tightened. I wanted to jump up and run. But I stayed. I stayed for five seconds. Then ten. Then I stopped and let myself breathe.

That was a win. Not because I felt anything good. But because I felt something. And I didn't die.

Wild, right? That's how low the bar is. Feeling your feet without dying. But that's where recovery lives. In the tiny victories that nobody else can see.

Your body needs proof that sensation is safe. It needs repeated, gentle, non-threatening experiences of pleasure before it will trust you to handle more. You can't argue your way out of a trauma response. You can only show your body, again and again, that it's okay to feel.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey. It shifts the question from "what's wrong with you" to "what happened to you," which is a much more useful way to approach the return of sensation.

Pleasure as a practice, not a destination

I used to think that one day I would wake up and be healed. That pleasure would return like a switch being flipped. That I would suddenly know how to enjoy things like normal people do.

That's not how it works. The return of pleasure is a practice. It's something you have to do on purpose, every day, even when it feels fake. Even when it feels like you're pretending. Even when your body is screaming at you to stop.

You have to court pleasure like a skittish animal. You can't chase it. You can't demand it. You have to sit still and wait for it to come to you. And sometimes it won't. And that's okay. You try again tomorrow.

I started with one thing every day. One moment of intentional pleasure. It could be the first sip of coffee. The feeling of hot water in the shower. The texture of a soft sweater. I didn't try to feel anything. I just tried to notice. And I let whatever came up, come up.

Most days, nothing came up. But some days, something did. A tiny spark. A moment of warmth. A second where I forgot to be afraid. And those moments became the foundation.

Deb Dana, a clinician who specializes in polyvagal theory, calls this "glimmers." Small moments of safety and connection that your nervous system can learn to recognize and seek out. They're the opposite of triggers. And they're just as powerful.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It gives you exercises for tracking these small moments and building capacity for sensation over time.

The grief of what was lost

I have to be honest with you. The return of pleasure comes with grief. You will feel sad for all the years you missed. For the joy you couldn't access. For the relationships you couldn't feel. For the life you lived inside a glass box, watching everyone else feel things you couldn't reach.

That grief is real. And it deserves to be held. Don't skip it. Don't tell yourself you should just be grateful for the progress you've made. Let yourself mourn. Because that mourning is part of the healing.

I cried for three days when I first started feeling pleasure again. Not because I was happy. Because I was angry. Angry at what was taken from me. Angry at how long I had lived without knowing what I was missing. Angry at everyone who told me to just "think positive" when my nervous system was in survival mode.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab. Because part of protecting your growing capacity for pleasure is learning to say no to the things that overwhelm it.

You are allowed to be angry. You are allowed to be sad. You are allowed to grieve the numbness that kept you safe but also kept you small. And then you are allowed to keep going.

Where you might be right now

Maybe you're reading this and feeling hopeless. Maybe you've been trying to feel again for years and nothing has changed. Maybe you're wondering if you're the one person who's too broken to come back.

You're not. I promise you're not. The fact that you're still reading, still searching, still hoping for something different, that's proof that the numbness hasn't won. There's a part of you that still wants to feel. And that part is alive, even if it's buried deep.

The return of pleasure takes time. It takes patience. It takes a willingness to feel bad before you can feel good. And it takes a kind of courage that nobody talks about. The courage to feel nothing, over and over again, and still show up the next day and try again.

Your body will learn. It will remember how to feel. It will remember how to trust. Not because you forced it, but because you proved to it, day after day, that sensation is safe. That pleasure won't kill you. That you can handle whatever comes up.

And one day, maybe not today, maybe not tomorrow, you'll be sitting on your couch eating a piece of chocolate, and you'll notice something. You'll notice that you can taste it. That you can feel the warmth of the room. That you're present in your body. And that it's okay.

That's the slow return. It's not a miracle. It's not a switch. It's a thousand small moments of choosing to stay, choosing to feel, choosing to trust. And it's worth every second of the wait.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
  • What Happened to You? (paid link)
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link)
